body_txt: You are doing your duty — you know it, you have been instructed from childhood in how to behave properly. Or perhaps you are just listening to something that was done. Properly. So why do you feel like it was Dirty Business ? It is inexplicable, even stunning and shocking, to feel this way. You are Just Following Orders , and Loyalty is virtuous, isn't it? In this trope, a character's conscience, the inner voice of their moral compass, unexpectedly bothers or interrupts them — possibly for the first time. The smallest of things may give it a chance to shout through the noise. The most common cause is that the evil being done is to someone outside the purported scope of Moral Myopia . Although association with such people can trigger it, it is not required. Others may arise when a character raised to never give a sucker an even break feels guilty about cheating, or other times when cunning appears despicable. Frequently the character can articulate all sorts of reasons why they should not feel this way, plenty of excuses to ignore that pesky conscience. However, the excuses are weak and the character's conflict manifests in mute failure to defy the qualms. More exaggerated cases may believe Bad Is Good and Good Is Bad , which makes it even more confusing to question one's moral stance. May overlap with To Be Lawful or Good , when the moral dilemma is more complex. Compare Conscience Makes You Go Back when the character fully intends not to do a good deed, but (to their annoyance) turns around to do it anyway. It may prompt a Face Realization as the character recognizes they always had this inner voice, but ignored or dismissed it until now. If they obey it this time, they may decide Screw the Rules, I'm Doing What's Right! . Ignored Epiphany is what ensues when the character dismisses the qualms, decides to ignore their conscience, and learns nothing.
